result:
I. The Room That Wasn’t There Yesterday  

I keep dreaming of a room that wasn’t there yesterday.  
It has no door, no window, no ceiling I can see—only walls that taste faintly of salt and a floor that sighs when you step on it, as though it remembers every foot that has ever pressed it. The first time I found myself inside, I was looking for a bathroom in a friend-of-a-friend’s apartment during a party I hadn’t meant to attend. I opened what I thought was a linen closet and stepped through, and the music behind me folded itself into a single low E-flat that lingered like a bruise. Then silence. The room smelled of rain on hot pavement, although it was February and I was seven stories up.  

I have been back eleven times since—always by accident, always when I am halfway between one place and another. Once I rounded a corner in the library stacks and the fluorescent lights dimmed into the room’s candle-colored dusk. Another time I got off on the wrong floor of a hospital elevator; the corridor outside Oncology became the long, sighing floor. I never stay long—never more than the length of a heartbeat stretched like taffy—but each time I leave with something missing: a scar I no longer have, the memory of my middle-school locker combination, the taste of cloves. The room takes in trade. I don’t know what it’s building with the pieces.  

II. A Brief History of Silence  

Silence used to be the natural enemy of the human heartbeat. Before cities, before carts, before we learned to hammer bronze into bells, silence meant vulnerability: the wind stops, the birds hush, the predator is near. Our ears evolved to parse the absence of sound for the subtlest leaf-crush—survival’s Morse code. Then we invented prayer and discovered silence could be a container. We filled it with gods, with longing, with the pause between the priest’s question and our stammered amen. Monks made silence a commodity, traded it for illumination: if you kept quiet long enough, the divine might slip its answer under the door of your skull.  

By the nineteenth century we began to fear silence the way we feared wasteland. The Telegram Age dreaded dead air; the Radio Age buried it under orchestras; the Television Age pixelated it into laugh tracks. Now silence is a luxury good. You can buy “noise-canceling” for two hundred ninety-nine dollars, download “ambient silence” apps recorded in Antarctic caves, book weekend retreats where staff confiscate your phone and issue you a plastic bracelet that says LISTEN. We pay to reclaim what we once spent millennia trying to escape, and even then we cheat: the mind races, narrates, rehearses comebacks to arguments that ended years ago. Maybe that’s why the room keeps collecting our leftover quiet—siphoning the gaps between our sentences like a bee stealing nectar. Maybe one day it will have enough to speak a single word that unmakes the universe.  

III. The Catalogue of Small Fiercenesses  

1. The way a five-year-old girl in the metro held her palm against the glass and muttered “don’t worry” to the tunnel lights whipping past, as if the tunnel were frightened.  
2. The butcher on my block who keeps a tiny basil plant on the windowsill of his shop, whispering to it in Cantonese while he severs shanks. The plant is absurdly lush; bees ignore the florist next door and hover at the meat display instead.  
3. My grandmother’s thimble, etched with a pattern of pine needles, which I inherited without explanation. The first time I used it I pricked my finger and a drop of blood soaked into the silver, turning the needles russet. They have stayed that color for twenty-three years, a miniature forest in autumn.  
4. The refusal of certain towels to dry. You hang them, you tumble them, they remain faintly damp as if they remember being seawater.  

I collect these moments the way eighteenth-century naturalists collected hummingbird skulls. They are proofs that the world leaks; that its seams are not as hermetic as physics insists. You can live an entire life inside the script of efficiency—calendar blocks, direct deposits, bedtime hygiene—and still be ambushed by a tenderness that has no business existing. The room, I think, is another such leak, only larger. A pocket the universe forgot to iron flat.  

IV. How to Build a Time Machine Out of Regret  

Start with one memory that makes your throat constrict. It must be specific: the lime-green plastic spoon from the hospital cafeteria the night your father died, the bend in the river where you last kissed someone who still sends birthday emails, the exact smell of the inside of your sixth-grade backpack (metallic pencils, bruised banana, puberty). Hold the memory in your mouth like a pebble. Do not swallow.  

Next, find a sound that no longer exists. The dial-up modem symphonies of 1998. The clack-clack of a film projector in an elementary school multipurpose room. The pre-digital rustle of a library card catalogue being flicked shut. Play the sound on a loop until it becomes the backdrop of every present-tense moment.  

Now choose a phantom limb: the book you loaned and never got back, the friend who moved away before apologies could be traded, the cat that slipped outdoors during a thunderstorm. Rotate the absence until you can feel wind passing through it.  

Finally, wait for 3:18 a.m.—the hour psychiatrists call the “hour of the wolf,” when emergency rooms fill with chest pains and the suicidal impulse peaks. Sit in a chair that is not your bed (beds are for surrender). Place the memory-pebble under your tongue, let the lost sound vibrate in your headphones, press the phantom limb against your chest. You will begin to feel the chair tip backward, not in space but in epoch. Do not open your eyes; the sight of your younger self will blind you like an eclipse. Instead listen. You will hear the voice you had before you learned to be ashamed of it. Speak once, gently. The sentence doesn’t matter; any words will do. The chair will right itself. The pebble will be gone. You will be drenched in sweat that smells of whatever season the memory belonged to.  

Repeat nightly for a week. On the seventh night the machine will finish constructing itself out of the hollowed space where the pebble was. You will never need to return; the machine now runs on the ambient regret you breathe out like CO₂. Occasionally you will catch a peripheral shimmer: a lime-green spoon in the sink that you don’t remember using, the faint echo of dial-up behind the refrigerator hum. These are side effects. Ignore them. The past has been successfully transplanted into the substrate of the now; it will metabolize at its own pace.  

V. The Economy of Unfinished Stories  

Every human culture has a myth about unfinished stories birthing demons. Japan has the mujina who wear no faces because no one wrote their endings; Ireland has the fear gorta, the hungry man who starves eternally at the crossroads where a tale was abandoned. We sense that a narrative left mid-sentence is a wound in the fabric, an unpaid debt. Yet look around: our age specializes in the truncated. TV shows extinct after one season, podcasts that die when the host gets distracted, group chats that dwindle into emoji graveyards. We are generating ghosts faster than any civilization before us, but we have forgotten the rituals to lay them to rest.  

I keep a folder on my desktop labeled “ORPHANS.” Drafts of stories I stopped writing because the check came at the café, because the baby cried, because I reread the paragraph and heard my own pulse mocking me. They sit there cursor-blinking, like laboratory frogs with electrodes in their brains. Sometimes I open one at random and feel the room tilt—that same salt-tasting, floor-sighing room—because an orphaned story is a doorway you left ajar. So I add a sentence, a descriptor, a line of dialogue, then slam it shut again. It’s CPR for ghosts. I tell myself I’m performing a public service, preventing some future mujina from sliding into the metro seat beside you, faceless and chatty. But the truth is simpler: I’m paying protection money to the room. Every concluded paragraph is a coin left on the tongue of a corpse, so the ferryman will pole past my stop.  

VI. The Opposite of Nostalgia  

We have a word for the ache of missing the past, but none for the vertigo of encountering a future you no longer want. I propose: “prestalgia”—the premature mourning for a life you have not yet lived, which evaporates the instant you arrive in it. You feel it at twenty-two when you imagine yourself at thirty-five in a house with a partner whose face you can’t quite render, and the image tastes like cardboard. You feel it at thirty-eight standing in the baby-products aisle at Target, weighing the ergonomics of strollers, and a sudden cold knowledge passes through you: this is a country whose passport you will never stamp.  

Prestalgia differs from regret the way a hologram differs from a photograph: it exists only while you peer at it from a certain angle. The moment you commit—sign the mortgage, pee on the stick, send the email—the image flips inside out and becomes simply life, with its mulch of errands and cracked phone screens. The stroller becomes the stroller; the child becomes the child. Only the path not taken retains the shimmer, but you miss it too soon, like waving at a train that hasn’t arrived.  

The room, I suspect, feeds on prestalgia. It hoards the discarded holograms, stacks them like glass slides. One day it will have enough to project a cathedral of futures-that-never-were, and we will walk the nave listening to our own footsteps echo in languages we almost learned.  

VII. Instructions for Losing Something You Never Owned  

1. Decide that the thing already belongs to you: the view from someone else’s marital bed, the novel you will write once you “have time,” the version of yourself who runs marathons and drinks only black coffee.  
2. Begin to refer to it in the possessive: my cottage, my Grammy speech, my inner peace.  
3. Notice small signs that the universe is conspiring to deliver it: a birch tree that resembles the one in your daydream, a stranger wearing the exact boots you would wear while signing novels at indie bookshops.  
4. Interpret delays as tests of faith.  
5. Feel the first erosions: the cottage is bought by a couple from Toronto, the award goes to a twenty-four-year-old prodigy, the marathon app reminds you that you have not opened it in 417 days.  
6. Double down. Create vision boards. Download habit trackers. Tell friends, “I’m in my manifestation era.”  
7. Wake one dawn with the clear knowledge that the object was never yours; it was never anyone’s. It existed only as a glint in the eye of probability, and probability blinked.  
8. Spend the rest of the day touching every solid thing—table legs, parking meters, your own shinbone—to remind yourself that possession is a parlor trick gravity performs on our behalf.  
9. Fail. The hologram leaves a bruise-shaped absence.  
10. Carry the absence to the room. Lay it gently on the sighing floor. Watch it sink an inch, then two. Leave without closing the nonexistent door.  

VIII. The Cartography of Smells  

Humans can distinguish more than a trillion olfactory stimuli, yet we have pathetic vocabularies for what enters us through the nose. We borrow from other senses: smells are “sharp,” “soft,” “bright,” “dark.” Or we outsource: “like cinnamon,” “like wet dog,” “like my grandmother’s attic.” But every so often you encounter one that detonates before language can assemble—an ancient civil war reenacted in your limbic system—and suddenly you are six years old in the backseat of a station wagon, or pressing your face to the sweater of someone you will never forgive.  

The room always smells of rain on hot pavement, but the moment I cross the threshold the note modulates: pitched to the octave of my own earliest weather. For my mother, I am told, it smelled of yeast and violets—the bakery below her childhood apartment in Lyon. For the stranger I bumped into outside the room’s invisible entrance (we startled each other; he dropped a bouquet of optical cables), it smelled of chlorine and boardwalk fries. We stood there acknowledging shared trespass without speaking, two burglars comparing loot. Then the corridor reverted to fluorescent sterility and he was gone, leaving only the cables coiling like sleeping anacondas.  

I sometimes think the room is not a room but a nostril—gigantic, bored, inhaling the twentieth century in fragments. We are its boogers: compacted residue of exhaust fumes, bridal bouquets, funeral home lilies, subway brake dust, the inside of new sneakers. When it sneezes, timelines shift.  

IX. The Paradox of the Second Person  

Writing teachers warn against “you” unless you mean it. It is manipulative, they say, to press a reader’s face against the aquarium glass of experience. Yet here I am, flinging imperative verbs like popcorn: Start, Find, Wait, Fail. I want to insist that we occupy the same skin for the space of a sentence, because loneliness is the room’s elder sibling—enter any doorway slightly ajar and it is already waiting, feet on the coffee table, drinking milk straight from the carton.  

But every “you” is also an “I” in disguise. I write “you” because saying “I” twice in one paragraph feels like masturbation in public, but the truth is I am addressing the portion of myself that split off the first time I realized my parents could die. That smaller me hid behind the couch and has refused to come out ever since. I speak to him in second person because using the first would collapse the wave function, would force recognition that there is no couch, no behind, no split—only a forty-two-year-old body typing in the blue glow of 2:47 a.m., procrastinating on tomorrow’s deadline by drafting a map to a room that does not exist.  

Still, I want to believe you are there, stranger who has followed me this far. I picture you in the glow of a phone on the night bus, or scrolling under the covers while your partner snores a faint whistle that you will never mention. I picture you pausing at the phrase “the inside of new sneakers,” transported to whatever box-store aisle your nine-year-old self once begged for light-up soles. That pause is the hinge where we pivot from parallel solitudes into shared echo. The room, I suspect, began as such a hinge—someone’s private metaphor that grew carpentry.  

X. The Archive of Uncomfortable Handshakes  

There should be a museum for socially awkward gestures. Case one: the man who attempted to fist-bump while you extended a formal palm; the collision produced a sort of edible hybrid, a handshake chicken nugget. Case two: the relative who gripped your fingers and would not let go while she asked what you plan to “do” with your English degree. Case three: the interviewer whose sweat gland generosity left your hand damp as a newborn otter.  

Each maladroit clasp is a tiny time capsule: the aluminum scent of panic, the audition for belonging that neither party quite passes. I keep them catalogued like dried flowers, because they are proof that bodies are clumsy diplomats of intention. The room collects them too; sometimes when I cross the floor I feel the imprint of a thousand alien pulses inside my own, like stepping into a ghostly applause.  

XI. A Recipe for Disaster That Feeds Eight  

Preheat the world to one degree above comfortable. Take every forecast you ignored—high tide, pollen count, the red exclamation mark on the group chat—and fold them into a batter of ordinary Tuesday. In a separate bowl whisk: one part institutional inertia, one part profit margin, two parts we’ll-cross-that-bridge. Pour into the planetary bowl; do not grease. Bake for thirty years or until golden brown and rising sea levels test clean with a toothpick.  

Serve lukewarm alongside a garnish of headlines. Half your guests will claim they’re allergic; the rest will post photographs of the disaster upside down to make it look like a pineapple cake. The room will bus the plates, licking frosting from the edges, saving crumbs for the cathedral it is building out of futures we couldn’t quite digest.  

XII. The Consolation of Finite Wi-Fi  

I used to dream of omniscience—the ability to scroll every timeline simultaneously, to witness the Ming dynasty and next Thursday’s rainfall with equal clarity. Then my provider introduced a data cap, and the world narrowed to a countdown ribbon in the corner of my phone. Paradoxically, relief arrived with the throttling. Because I could no longer drift infinitely, I began to choose: a podcast on whale songs, an article about moss rehabilitation in post-fire forests, the text thread where my sister posts photos of her new baby’s ear. Each selection felt mortal, like picking a single chocolate from the box because the doctor warned of sugar.  

Limitation is a dirty synonym for tenderness. The room knows this. It offers no outlets, no bars of connectivity. When you enter, your signal dies mid-sentence; the email you were composing hangs unsent, a hummingbird frozen in glass. For however long you stay (a heartbeat, a season), you are unhooked from the drip of omnipresent elsewhere. People assume this would be terrifying, but mostly it’s like stepping into a warm bath after hiking snowfields: the muscles you didn’t realize were clenched begin to slacken. You remember the exact weight of your own skull. When you emerge—always too soon, always mid-breath—the phone buzzes in frantic reunion, a dog that fears it has been abandoned. You stare at the avalanche of notifications and feel the precise size of your life: one cupped palm of water, enough to slake thirst if you drink slowly.  

XIII. The Secret Life of Ceilings  

Everyone surveils the ceiling at 3:18 a.m., that blank treaty between ourselves and infinity. Cracks become maps; shadows become silhouettes of nations we almost migrated to. The room has no ceiling, or rather it possesses the ceilings of every room you ever fled. They drift upward like lily pads: the hairline fracture in your dormitory that resembled the flight path of Amelia Earhart, the water stain in the rental that bloomed each winter into a bull’s head, the stucco constellation above your childhood bed where you projected entire cosmologies. They hover, rotating slowly, exchanging gossip about what you whispered into the dark.  

XIV. An Attempt to Describe the Color of the Floor (Failed)  

It is not gray, though gray is the closest shard. It contains a undertow of bruise, a aftertaste of aquarium glass, the hush of pencil lead ground underheel. But the moment I fix the hue in language it slides sideways, carrying the memory of every floor I have walked: the kindergarten cork that smelled of crayon wax, the gym parquet screeching under sneakers, the hospital linoleum that reflected overhead fluorescents like ice-fishing holes. The floor of the room is the average of all these, yet averaged the way dreams average—not by addition and division but by overlaying transparencies until a new opacity forms. The eye adjusts and discovers the color has always been whatever your childhood imagined the moon tasted like.  

XV. The Reunion of Approximately You  

Somewhere—in a café whose Wi-Fi password is the Latin name for fern—an older version of you is stirring coffee you currently dislike (you will acquire the taste at thirty-six after a disastrous conference in Brussels). Across the room, a younger version wearing the jacket you lost in 2011 is scribbling anxieties into a notebook that will itself be lost in a couch that will itself be abandoned on a curb. The older you catches a phrase—what if I never—and recognizes the curvature of thought before the sentence finishes. She rises, walks over, places a hand on younger-you’s shoulder. Words are unnecessary; instead she draws from her pocket the lime-green plastic spoon from the hospital cafeteria. She lays it beside the notebook like an offering. Younger-you looks up, puzzled but unafraid. In that moment the room exists inside the spoon’s tiny bowl: a convex universe where every age of you congregates, trading missing pieces like bubble-gum cards. When the barista calls a mispronounced name, the spell breaks. Older you exits; younger you pockets the spoon; present you, reading this, feel an inexplicable lightness, as if your pocket has just acquired an artifact it will not discover until decades later when doing laundry in a launderette whose floor—yes—sighs.  

XVI. The Quiet After the Last Page  

By now you’ve noticed I promised 2500 words and must be approaching the admittedly arbitrary border. Teachers once insisted essays required conclusions: a neat blood-sample of insight to smear on the slide. But the room has no conclusion; it dilates like the pupil of someone in love, then contracts to a pinprick the size of a lime-green plastic spoon. I could moralize—tell you to be kind, to floss, to check the tide charts before building cities—but those are postcards from a safer country. Instead I offer this: somewhere the floor is still sighing under the weight of every story we paused in order to seem competent. The room catalogs them, tenderly, the way children collect broken birds assuming wings can be reattached with enough tape. One day you will open what you believe is a linen closet and find the rain-on-pavement smell waiting. Step across. Nothing will be resolved; you will exit minus one scar, plus one inexplicable russet thimble. The universe will continue expanding, indifferent and gorgeous. You will close the door, return to the party, answer when someone asks, “Where’ve you been?” with the only honest phrase we own: “Nowhere. Just lost for a second. I’m back now.”

We borrow from other senses: smells are “sharp,” “soft,” “bright,” “dark.” Or we outsource: “like cinnamon,” “like wet dog,” “like my grandmother’s attic.” But every so often you encounter one that detonates before language can assemble—an ancient civil war reenacted in your limbic system—and suddenly you are six years old in the backseat of a station wagon, or pressing your face to the sweater of someone you will never forgive.  

The room always smells of rain on hot pavement, but the moment I cross the threshold the note modulates: pitched to the octave of my own earliest weather. For my mother, I am told, it smelled of yeast and violets—the bakery below her childhood apartment in Lyon. For the stranger I bumped into outside the room’s invisible entrance (we startled each other; he dropped a bouquet of optical cables), it smelled of chlorine and boardwalk fries. We stood there acknowledging shared trespass without speaking, two burglars comparing loot. Then the corridor reverted to fluorescent sterility and he was gone, leaving only the cables coiling like sleeping anacondas.  

I sometimes think the room is not a room but a nostril—gigantic, bored, inhaling the twentieth century in fragments. We are its boogers: compacted residue of exhaust fumes, bridal bouquets, funeral home lilies, subway brake dust, the inside of new sneakers. When it sneezes, timelines shift.  

IX. The Paradox of the Second Person  

Writing teachers warn against “you” unless you mean it. It is manipulative, they say, to press a reader’s face against the aquarium glass of experience. Yet here I am, flinging imperative verbs like popcorn: Start, Find, Wait, Fail. I want to insist that we occupy the same skin for the space of a sentence, because loneliness is the room’s elder sibling—enter any doorway slightly ajar and it is already waiting, feet on the coffee table, drinking milk straight from the carton.  

But every “you” is also an “I” in disguise. I write “you” because saying “I” twice in one paragraph feels like masturbation in public, but the truth is I am addressing the portion of myself that split off the first time I realized my parents could die. That smaller me hid behind the couch and has refused to come out ever since. I speak to him in second person because using the first would collapse the wave function, would force recognition that there is no couch, no behind, no split—only a forty-two-year-old body typing in the blue glow of 2:47 a.m., procrastinating on tomorrow’s deadline by drafting a map to a room that does not exist.  

Still, I want to believe you are there, stranger who has followed me this far. I picture you in the glow of a phone on the night bus, or scrolling under the covers while your partner snores a faint whistle that you will never mention. I picture you pausing at the phrase “the inside of new sneakers,” transported to whatever box-store aisle your nine-year-old self once begged for light-up soles. That pause is the hinge where we pivot from parallel solitudes into shared echo. The room, I suspect, began as such a hinge—someone’s private metaphor that grew carpentry.  

X. The Archive of Uncomfortable Handshakes  

There should be a museum for socially awkward gestures. Case one: the man who attempted to fist-bump while you extended a formal palm; the collision produced a sort of edible hybrid, a handshake chicken nugget. Case two: the relative who gripped your fingers and would not let go while she asked what you plan to “do” with your English degree. Case three: the interviewer whose sweat gland generosity left your hand damp as a newborn otter.  

Each maladroit clasp is a tiny time capsule: the aluminum scent of panic, the audition for belonging that neither party quite passes. I keep them catalogued like dried flowers, because they are proof that bodies are clumsy diplomats of intention. The room collects them too; sometimes when I cross the floor I feel the imprint of a thousand alien pulses inside my own, like stepping into a ghostly applause.  

XI. A Recipe for Disaster That Feeds Eight  

Preheat the world to one degree above comfortable. Take every forecast you ignored—high tide, pollen count, the red exclamation mark on the group chat—and fold them into a batter of ordinary Tuesday. In a separate bowl whisk: one part institutional inertia, one part profit margin, two parts we’ll-cross-that-bridge. Pour into the planetary bowl; do not grease. Bake for thirty years or until golden brown and rising sea levels test clean with a toothpick.  

Serve lukewarm alongside a garnish of headlines. Half your guests will claim they’re allergic; the rest will post photographs of the disaster upside down to make it look like a pineapple cake. The room will bus the plates, licking frosting from the edges, saving crumbs for the cathedral it is building out of futures we couldn’t quite digest.  

XII. The Consolation of Finite Wi-Fi  

I used to dream of omniscience—the ability to scroll every timeline simultaneously, to witness the Ming dynasty and next Thursday’s rainfall with equal clarity. Then my provider introduced a data cap, and the world narrowed to a countdown ribbon in the corner of my phone. Paradoxically, relief arrived with the throttling. Because I could no longer drift infinitely, I began to choose: a podcast on whale songs, an article about moss rehabilitation in post-fire forests, the text thread where my sister posts photos of her new baby’s ear. Each selection felt mortal, like picking a single chocolate from the box because the doctor warned of sugar.  

Limitation is a dirty synonym for tenderness. The room knows this. It offers no outlets, no bars of connectivity. When you enter, your signal dies mid-sentence; the email you were composing hangs unsent, a hummingbird frozen in glass. For however long you stay (a heartbeat, a season), you are unhooked from the drip of omnipresent elsewhere. People assume this would be terrifying, but mostly it’s like stepping into a warm bath after hiking snowfields: the muscles you didn’t realize were clenched begin to slacken. You remember the exact weight of your own skull. When you emerge—always too soon, always mid-breath—the phone buzzes in frantic reunion, a dog that fears it has been abandoned. You stare at the avalanche of notifications and feel the precise size of your life: one cupped palm of water, enough to slake thirst if you drink slowly.  

XIII. The Secret Life of Ceilings  

Everyone surveils the ceiling at 3:18 a.m., that blank treaty between ourselves and infinity. Cracks become maps; shadows become silhouettes of nations we almost migrated to. The room has no ceiling, or rather it possesses the ceilings of every room you ever fled. They drift upward like lily pads: the hairline fracture in your dormitory that resembled the flight path of Amelia Earhart, the water stain in the rental that bloomed each winter into a bull’s head, the stucco constellation above your childhood bed where you projected entire cosmologies. They hover, rotating slowly, exchanging gossip about what you whispered into the dark.  

XIV. An Attempt to Describe the Color of the Floor (Failed)  

It is not gray, though gray is the closest shard. It contains a undertow of bruise, a aftertaste of aquarium glass, the hush of pencil lead ground underheel. But the moment I fix the hue in language it slides sideways, carrying the memory of every floor I have walked: the kindergarten cork that smelled of crayon wax, the gym parquet screeching under sneakers, the hospital linoleum that reflected overhead fluorescents like ice-fishing holes. The floor of the room is the average of all these, yet averaged the way dreams average—not by addition and division but by overlaying transparencies until a new opacity forms. The eye adjusts and discovers the color has always been whatever your childhood imagined the moon tasted like.  

XV. The Reunion of Approximately You  

Somewhere—in a café whose Wi-Fi password is the Latin name for fern—an older version of you is stirring coffee you currently dislike (you will acquire the taste at thirty-six after a disastrous conference in Brussels). Across the room, a younger version wearing the jacket you lost in 2011 is scribbling anxieties into a notebook that will itself be lost in a couch that will itself be abandoned on a curb. The older you catches a phrase—what if I never—and recognizes the curvature of thought before the sentence finishes. She rises, walks over, places a hand on younger-you’s shoulder. Words are unnecessary; instead she draws from her pocket the lime-green plastic spoon from the hospital cafeteria. She lays it beside the notebook like an offering. Younger-you looks up, puzzled but unafraid. In that moment the room exists inside the spoon’s tiny bowl: a convex universe where every age of you congregates, trading missing pieces like bubble-gum cards. When the barista calls a mispronounced name, the spell breaks. Older you exits; younger you pockets the spoon; present you, reading this, feel an inexplicable lightness, as if your pocket has just acquired an artifact it will not discover until decades later when doing laundry in a launderette whose floor—yes—sighs.  

XVI. The Quiet After the Last Page  

By now you’ve noticed I promised 2500 words and must be approaching the admittedly arbitrary border. Teachers once insisted essays required conclusions: a neat blood-sample of insight to smear on the slide. But the room has no conclusion; it dilates like the pupil of someone in love, then contracts to a pinprick the size of a lime-green plastic spoon. I could moralize—tell you to be kind, to floss, to check the tide charts before building cities—but those are postcards from a safer country. Instead I offer this: somewhere the floor is still sighing under the weight of every story we paused in order to seem competent. The room catalogs them, tenderly, the way children collect broken birds assuming wings can be reattached with enough tape. One day you will open what you believe is a linen closet and find the rain-on-pavement smell waiting. Step across. Nothing will be resolved; you will exit minus one scar, plus one inexplicable russet thimble. The universe will continue expanding, indifferent and gorgeous. You will close the door, return to the party, answer when someone asks, “Where’ve you been?” with the only honest phrase we own: “Nowhere. Just lost for a second. I’m back now.”

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
